required.title: [Read Aloud] The Berenstain Bears and the Trouble with Neighbors
required.author: Stan and Jan Berenstain

required.title: [Read Aloud] Last Stop on Market Street
required.author: Matt de la Peña

required.title: [Materials] Large paper and colored pencils or markers

supplementary.title: [For Mentor] Building Communities from the Inside Out
supplementary.author: John P. Kretzmann and John L. McKnight

supplementary.title: [Activity] A clipboard, pencil, and walking shoes

competence_markers.description: The child can name at least five neighbors
They can draw a basic map of their neighborhood from memory and observation
They can identify at least three community helpers by name and role
They can articulate one thing in their community that they think could be improved
They demonstrate the habit of noticing — pointing out things in their environment that they would not have noticed before this packet

narration_prompts.detail: Describe what you saw on your neighborhood walk — the buildings, the green spaces, the things that stood out to you.
Tell me about at least three neighbors by name. What do you know about them?
Name at least three people — by name or by role — who help others in our area. What do they do?

narration_prompts.prompt: What is one thing in our neighborhood that could be better?
narration_prompts.detail: Describe it. Why do you think it matters?

narration_prompts.prompt: Tell me something you learned about the story of our neighborhood.
narration_prompts.detail: What was here before? What has changed?

cross_connections.description: The needs awareness developed here feeds directly into the service work of 4.5.2. The child who can see what needs doing is ready to start doing it.
The physical features the child maps here — parks, roads, fire stations — are all the result of government decisions explored in 4.5.3.
The neighborhood mapping skills are a local-scale application of the geographic awareness developed in 1.2.
Meeting neighbors and learning their stories exercises the listening and social skills developed in 3.3.
Knowing your neighborhood — who lives there, what resources exist, how to navigate it — is both civic competence and practical preparedness.

content.why_this_matters:
You live somewhere specific. Not "somewhere in the world" — a real place, with real people, real buildings, real streets, and real stories. Most people walk through their neighborhood every day without really seeing it. They do not know who lives three houses down. They do not know the name of the person who runs the library or the fire station. They drive past places every day and never wonder what goes on inside them.

This packet is about learning to pay attention. Not to faraway places — to the place you actually live. Because before you can help your community, before you can be part of it in a way that matters, you have to know it. You have to know who your neighbors are, what is in your neighborhood, who helps when things go wrong, and what could be better. That kind of noticing is where all civic life begins.

You are going to walk your neighborhood with new eyes. You are going to meet people you may have walked past a hundred times. You are going to draw a map not from a screen but from what you see with your own eyes. And when you are done, you will know your place in a way that most adults never do.
content.what_this_develops:
1. **The habit of noticing** — paying deliberate attention to the people and places in your immediate environment
2. **Neighbor awareness** — knowing who lives near you by name, face, and basic circumstance
3. **Spatial awareness of your neighborhood** — being able to describe and draw what is around you from observation and memory
4. **Understanding of community helpers** — identifying the people whose work keeps the community functioning
5. **Early needs awareness** — the ability to notice things that are broken, missing, or could be improved
6. **Place identity** — a sense of belonging to a specific place with a specific history
content.core_content:
### Part 1: You Live in a Real Place

Think about where you live. Not just your house or your apartment — the whole area around it. The street you live on. The streets nearby. The park, if there is one. The store where your family buys groceries. The school. The library. The place where you go when you need help.

All of these things together make up your neighborhood. And your neighborhood is not just buildings and roads. It is people. The woman who walks her dog every morning. The man who mows his lawn on Saturdays. The family with the trampoline in the backyard. The older couple who sit on their porch in the evenings. These are your neighbors, and knowing them — even a little — is one of the most important things a person can do.

For most of human history, people knew their neighbors. They had to. If your house caught fire, your neighbors helped put it out. If you ran out of food, a neighbor might share. If a stranger came to the area, neighbors told each other. People watched out for each other because they knew each other.

Today, many people do not know their neighbors at all. They come home, go inside, close the door, and never speak to the person next door. This is not normal in human history. It is new, and it is a loss. A neighborhood where people know each other is safer, friendlier, and stronger than one where people are strangers living side by side.

### Part 2: The Neighborhood Walk

You are going to take a walk through your neighborhood — but not a regular walk. This is an observation walk. Your job is to notice things you have never noticed before, even if you have walked this route a hundred times.

Here is what to look for:

**Homes.** How many homes can you see from your house? Are they houses, apartments, or something else? Do you know who lives in them? Can you see signs of the people who live there — a garden, a flag, toys in the yard, a car in the driveway?

**Buildings that are not homes.** What else is in your neighborhood? A school? A church or temple or mosque? A fire station? A library? A store? A restaurant? A post office? A community center? Make a list of every building you can identify and what it is for.

**Green spaces.** Is there a park? A playground? An empty lot with trees or grass? A community garden? A creek or pond? Where do people go outside in your neighborhood?

**Things that move.** What kinds of vehicles do you see? Are there buses? Bikes? Delivery trucks? Where do the roads go? Is there a sidewalk? Is it easy to walk around, or do you have to be careful of traffic?

**Things that need attention.** Is anything broken? A cracked sidewalk? A streetlight that does not work? Trash on the ground? A building that looks empty or run down? A park bench that needs paint? These are not complaints — they are observations. You are learning to see what is actually there, including the parts that could be better.

After your walk, you are going to draw a map. Not a perfect map — a map from your memory and your observations. Put your home in the middle. Draw the streets around it. Put in the buildings you noticed, the parks, the stores, the places that matter. Label everything you can. This is your neighborhood map, and you made it yourself.

### Part 3: Who Are Our Neighbors?

Now comes the most important part: the people.

Your mentor is going to help you learn the names of at least five neighbors. Not just "the people next door" — their actual names. You might already know some of them. For others, this might mean knocking on a door, saying hello, and introducing yourself. That can feel a little scary, and that is normal. Meeting new people takes courage, even for adults.

For each neighbor you meet or already know, try to learn a few things:
- What is their name?
- How long have they lived here?
- Do they have a family? Pets?
- What do they do during the day?
- Is there anything they need help with?

You do not need to write a report. You just need to know them — the way people have always known their neighbors. When you see them, you wave. When something happens, you check on them. When they need help, you notice.

This is the foundation of community. Not organizations, not programs, not government — people knowing each other by name and looking out for each other. Everything else in this pillar builds on this.

### Part 4: Who Helps in Our Community?

Every community has people whose job — or whose choice — is to help others. Some of them wear uniforms. Some do not. Learning who they are is part of knowing your community.

**People with helping jobs:**
- **Firefighters** respond when there is a fire, a car accident, or a medical emergency. Do you know where your nearest fire station is? Have you ever visited one?
- **Police officers** help keep people safe and respond when laws are broken. They are part of your community.
- **Librarians** help people find books, information, and resources. The library is one of the most valuable places in any community — it is free and open to everyone.
- **Teachers** help children learn. Your teachers are community helpers, even if you do not usually think of them that way.
- **Doctors and nurses** take care of people who are sick or hurt. Where is the nearest place to get medical help in your community?
- **Religious leaders** — pastors, priests, rabbis, imams — serve their congregations and often help the wider community as well.

**People who help by choice:**
Not every community helper has a title. Some of the most important helpers are just neighbors who pay attention. The neighbor who checks on the elderly woman down the street. The parent who organizes the block party. The teenager who shovels driveways after a snowstorm without being asked. The person who picks up trash in the park even though it is not their job.

These people are community helpers too. They help because they notice what needs doing and they do it. That is exactly the kind of person this packet is helping you become.

### Part 5: What Could Be Better?

Now that you have walked your neighborhood, drawn your map, met your neighbors, and identified the helpers — there is one more question: What could be better?

This is not about complaining. It is about seeing clearly. Every neighborhood has things that work well and things that do not. The park might be wonderful but the sidewalk might be crumbling. The library might be excellent but the nearest grocery store might be far away. The neighbors might be friendly but there might be no place for children to play safely.

Think about your neighborhood and pick one thing you think could be improved. It does not have to be something enormous. It might be:
- A park that needs cleaning up
- A streetlight that has been out for a long time
- A neighbor who seems lonely and could use a visit
- A place where it is hard to cross the street safely
- An empty lot that could be turned into something useful

You do not have to fix it right now. But noticing it — really seeing it and being able to describe it — is the first step. The person who sees what needs doing is the person who eventually does something about it. And that habit of seeing starts right here, right now, in your own neighborhood.

### Part 6: The Story of Your Place

Your neighborhood has a story. Every place does. Someone was there before you. Someone built the first house. Someone decided to put the school where it is. Someone planted the oldest tree.

Ask the people who have lived in your area the longest: What was this neighborhood like before? What has changed? What used to be here that is not here anymore? What is here now that was not here before?

You might hear stories about fields that became houses, about stores that opened and closed, about families that came and went, about events that everyone in the neighborhood remembers. These stories are the history of your place — not the kind of history that is in big books about presidents and wars, but the kind that belongs to the people who live where you live.

Knowing the story of your place makes you part of it. You are not just someone who happens to live there. You are someone who belongs there, who knows where you are, and who cares about what happens next.

mentor_guide.competence_evaluation:
### 1. Neighbor Knowledge
**Developing well:** The child can name five or more neighbors and share basic information about them. They wave or greet neighbors when they see them. They show genuine interest in the people around them.

**Developing slowly:** The child cannot name neighbors despite multiple walks and introductions. They show discomfort or disinterest in meeting people.

**Diagnostic questions:** Are you modeling neighborly behavior yourself? Have you actually knocked on doors together, or has this remained an intention? For shy children, start with neighbors you already know and build from there.

### 2. Spatial Awareness
**Developing well:** The child can draw a recognizable map of their neighborhood from memory. They can give basic directions to landmarks. They notice new things on familiar routes.

**Developing slowly:** The child cannot orient themselves in their own neighborhood. The map is vague or empty despite multiple walks.

**Diagnostic questions:** How many walks have you done? Spatial awareness builds through repetition. Are you pointing things out during walks and asking the child to point things out to you?

### 3. Community Helper Identification
**Developing well:** The child can name community helpers by role and, ideally, by personal name. They understand that help comes from both professional roles and personal choices.

**Developing slowly:** The child can list roles (firefighter, teacher) but has no personal connection to specific individuals in those roles.

**Diagnostic questions:** Have you visited the fire station, the library, or another community institution? Meeting a librarian by name is different from knowing that librarians exist.

### 4. Needs Awareness
**Developing well:** The child spontaneously points out things in the community that need attention — a broken fence, litter in a park, a neighbor who seems lonely. They frame these as things that could be addressed, not just problems.

**Developing slowly:** The child does not notice needs, or notices them only as complaints without any sense that they could be addressed.

**Diagnostic questions:** Are you modeling needs awareness? When you see a broken streetlight or an unkempt park, do you say so? The child learns this habit by watching you practice it.

### 5. Place Story
**Developing well:** The child can share at least one story about their neighborhood's history — something they learned from a neighbor, a family member, or local observation. They show a sense of belonging to the place.

**Developing slowly:** The child has no sense of the neighborhood's story. The place feels generic and interchangeable.

**Diagnostic questions:** Have you talked to long-time residents? Have you shared your own knowledge of the area's history? Stories are caught, not taught — the child needs to hear them from people who care about the place.
